title: Web Request Dispatcher (WRD)
html:
Web Request Dispatcher (WRD)

Add to My Topics
Add selected topic and subtopicsAdd Topic & Subtopics with topic headAdd entire publication
Watch
Download PDF
Save selected topicSave selected topic and subtopicsSave entire publication
Share
Email LinkCopy Link
Feedback
  • Last UpdatedSept 15, 2024
    • Uniface
    • 10.4

The Uniface Web Request Dispatcher (WRD) is used to deploy Uniface web applications. It is a Java servlet that is responsible for brokering requests and maintaining connections between web servers and the Uniface Router. The WRD communicates with the Uniface Server via the Uniface Router.

The WRD receives requests from a client browser via the web server and dispatches these requests to Uniface via TCP or TLS, depending on how the WRD is configured. A connection is established to the Uniface Router, which assigns the request to the Uniface Server for processing.

The WRD handles the HTTP request methods including GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, and OPTIONS. The HEAD request method works in a same way as a standard Java HTTP servlet. For OPTIONS request method, it returns a response with Allow header to show which HTTP methods it supports. This header value includes GET, HEAD, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE, and OPTIONS. For HTTP request methods GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE, it brokers these requests and dispatches them to Uniface Server for further processing by DSP, USP, or service components. For details, see $webinfo and INPUT.

The WRD 7.0 (and newer versions) runs in a Jakarta EE (Enterprise Edition) web container of a Jakarta EE-compliant web server (example, Apache Tomcat 10.1). It has the following features:

  • It is based on Jarkarta Servlet 6.0, whose package name is jakarta.servlet.
  • The Java modules in it are in package com.rocketsoftware.uniface.

The WRD 6.4 (and older versions) runs in a Java EE (Enterprise Edition) web container of a Java EE-compliant web server (example, Apache Tomcat 9). It has the following features:

  • It is based on Java Servlet 3.0, whose package name is javax.servlet.
  • The Java modules in it are in package com.compuware.uniface.

The web server handles a pool of WRD servlet instances and typically limits their number. All servlet instances communicate with the Uniface Router that handles the Uniface Server pools.

The WRD servlet is delivered in the Java archive file wrd.jar, which also includes the SOAP Request Dispatcher (SRD) servlet.

Note: In this version of document, all descriptions and examples are targeted WRD 7.0 or newer, that is with package names jakarta.servlet and com.rocketsoftware.uniface. For WRD 6.0, you need to replace these package names with javax.servlet and com.compuware.uniface.
Related Links
  • Java Servlets

    The Java™ Servlet API is a standard extension to Java that extends the functionality of a web server. Java servlets run as Java EE (Enterprise Edition) web containers within a Java EE-compliant web server.

  • SOAP Request Dispatcher (SRD)

    The Uniface SOAP Request Dispatcher (SRD) is a Java servlet that is responsible for brokering SOAP and WSDL requests for web services.

  • wrd.jar

    The Java archive file wrd.jar contains the Uniface Java servlets—the Web Request Dispatcher (WRD) for deploying web applications and SOAP Request Dispatcher (SRD) for SOAP-based web services.

  • Deploying Web Application Resources

    In a J2EE-compliant web server, deploy the web resources used by the Uniface web application.

  • TLS Connections for Uniface Servlets (WRD and SRD)

    The TLS connector supports encrypted network connections between the Uniface Router and Uniface servlets—the Web Request Dispatcher (WRD) and SOAP Request Dispatcher (SRD). The servlet acts as a TLS client, which means the certificate of the Uniface Router must be verified before the connection can be established.
